original_abstract: We deal with embedding a large scale knowledge graph composed of entities and relations into a continuous vector space. TransE is a promising method proposed recently, which is very efficient while achieving state-of-the-art predictive performance. We discuss some mapping properties of relations which should be considered in embedding, such as reflexive, one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many. We note that TransE does not do well in dealing with these properties. Some complex models are capable of preserving these mapping properties but sacrifice efficiency in the process. To make a good trade-off between model capacity and efficiency, in this paper we propose TransH which models a relation as a hyperplane together with a translation operation on it. In this way, we can well preserve the above mapping properties of relations with almost the same model complexity of TransE. Additionally, as a practical knowledge graph is often far from completed, how to construct negative examples to reduce false negative labels in training is very important. Utilizing the one-to-many/many-to-one mapping property of a relation, we propose a simple trick to reduce the possibility of false negative labeling. We conduct extensive experiments on link prediction, triplet classification and fact extraction on benchmark datasets like WordNet and Freebase. Experiments show TransH delivers significant improvements over TransE on predictive accuracy with comparable capability to scale up.
humanized_abstract: Embedding a large-scale knowledge graph into a continuous vector space has drawn much attention in recent years. A recently proposed model, called TransE, achieved both high accuracy and efficient embedding. In this paper, we discuss several important relation mapping properties that need to be taken into account, such as the reflexive, one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-one mapping relations. Unfortunately, however, the TransE model performs poorly in these relations. While more complex models have been proposed to meet these requirements, they tend to sacrifice efficiency. Therefore, to balance the model capacity and efficiency, in this paper, we propose the TransH model by modeling each relation as a hyperplane with a translation vector. The TransH model can effectively preserve the relation mapping properties and its complexity is almost the same as that of the TransE model. In addition, we propose a strategy for constructing negative instances, so as to decrease the amount of false negative samples in the training. Using the one-to-many or many-to-one relation mapping properties of a relation, we can propose a very simple method to minimize false negative samples. Experimental results are provided on link prediction, triplet classification and fact extraction tasks on several popular benchmarks such as WordNet and Freebase. Experimental results show that TransH performs much better than the TransE model, and can also handle large knowledge graphs.
